text: The surgeon makes four incisions for robotic trochars between the eighth and ninth right intercostal spaces. Using the da Vinci XI robotic system, the mediastinal pleura is incised, and the right upper pulmonary vein is identified and isolated with blunt dissection and electrocautery. Fenestrated bipolar forceps are used in the left hand, while the marrow gland is manipulated with the right hand, taking care to preserve the middle lobe vein.

The surgeon pulls the right upper lobe inferiorly, revealing the main pulmonary artery. A small hilar lymph node between the pulmonary artery and vein is isolated and removed. The pleura overlying the pulmonary artery is dissected, exposing the artery lateral to the superior vena cava. The right upper pulmonary vein is divided using a 30mm robotic vascular stapler, enhancing visualization of the pulmonary artery branches. The stump of the vein is lifted, and dissection of the pulmonary artery's adventitial plane exposes the Boyden branch parallel to the vein. Lymphadenectomy of Heiler station X is performed, aiding in the dissection of the Boyden branch's lower margin.

The surgeon divides the pulmonary artery using a 30mm robotic vascular stapler reload. The lung is pulled anteriorly and inferiorly to access the bronchial structures. Electrocautery is used to dissect the posterior pleural reflection, exposing the right upper lobe bronchus. Gentle blunt dissection frees the upper margin of the bronchus from the lower margin of the azygos vein. Lung tissue is cleared from the angle between the bronchus intermedius and the upper lobe bronchus, revealing the posterior hilar lymph nodes. The lobe is then pulled posteriorly.

The surgeon performs an accurate hilar lymphadenectomy, enabling dissection of the lower margin of the bronchus, which is transected using a robotic stapler with a 45mm green reload. The fissures between the upper, middle, and lower lobes are clearly defined, and division is achieved with multiple parenchymal reloads. Station seven lymphadenectomy is initiated by opening the posterior pleura from the superior edge of the lower lobe vein along the vagus nerve, providing safe visualization of the esophagus.

The surgeon approaches the paratracheal area by dissecting the mediastinal pleura above the azygos vein and along the lateral profile of the superior vena cava. Station IV-R and II-R lymph nodes are removed en bloc. The procedure concludes with the lobe being pulled out of the chest using a specimen retrieval bag through a utility incision created by joining the two middle robotic incisions.
